item.title: Just fixed a misspelling
item.content:encoded: From jounral to journal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get how you think interpreting the intentions of the author and comparing it to the result and your reaction is an unreasonable way for someone to read and criticise something.

I mean, you could go more in depth of the value or lack of value of authorial intent if you were a literary theorist, but on the casual level, 'you seemed to be trying to make me feel [x], I didn't feel [x], so this is not good' is a totally reasonable opinion.

To say something about the SCP itself, I thought the idea of the person being in this situation was ok, but the anomalous effects were overly weak - nothing about either the tunnel or the country itself (as it was so sparsely described) was interesting. Minor nitpicks: switching from city to country in "The passport contains stamps from airports located in Paris, Tokyo, The United States of America, and Germany" was off, and I don't think the "Paris International Airport" was a thing, as there was more than one airport with different names even at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get people that say "I see what you were trying to make me feel but I didn't feel it so the skip is bad".

It seems like there's only one comment in this discussion (Decibelles's) that mentioned "I see what you're going for but I didn't feel it", and the takeaway isn't "so the skip is bad". The comment is just that "saw the intent, didn't feel it".

You saw what he was trying to make you feel? Really? How do you know?

This seems overly accusatory, but if you have an issue with someone's response being on the vague side, you should probably reply directly to their comment when asking them to elaborate.

Your subjective perception about the supposed intentions of the author shouldn't taint your appreciation of the piece, in my humble opinion.

I don't think this is what happened. If the reader in question downvoted the piece, it doesn't seem to be due to their perception of the authorial intent. Decibelles stated, "overall it wasn't that exciting. It's written fairly enough but it failed to really connect with me much".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get people that say "I see what you were trying to make me feel but I didn't feel it so the skip is bad". You saw what he was trying to make you feel? Really? How do you know? Your subjective perception about the supposed intentions of the author shouldn't taint your appreciation of the piece, in my humble opinion. For me, this sounded like The Terminal, with Tom Hanks, but a thousand times worse, and that idea gave me the creeps. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, it seemed like he has returned, but the ashed returning are from his world, but not himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was going to no-vote, but that final line suddenly just wrapped the entire thing together in a way I can't describe. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That final line really got me for some reason.

Whatever, I'm upboating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know myself so I looked into it and found this on Wikipedia.

Gravity is usually measured in units of acceleration. In the SI system of units, the standard unit of acceleration is 1 metre per second squared (abbreviated as m/s2).

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravimetry#Units_of_measurement

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, so have an upvote!

One thing, though….

the force of gravity progressively increases from 9.81 m/s^2 to 15.32 m/s^2

Is that the right way to say that? I feel like it should say "from 1G to 1.5G" or "the acceleration due to gravity." I mean, m/s^2 isn't force, but I don't know what the right wording is…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good Idea, I've changed that sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially downvoted the first revision of this article, but have changed my vote to an upvote. It has a few problems (like why Taured itself isn't considered anomalous), but I found myself feeling so very fucking sorry for someone as lost as -1. From his point of view, this is the fucked up anomalous world. And yes, it's been explored in other articles, and this may not be the most perfect execution, but if you make me feel sorry and I want to keep reading, you get an upvote.

One thing though:

The passport contains stamps from airports located in Paris, Taured, Tokyo, The United States of America, and Germany, these stamps match the ones used by the associated country.

This sentence sounds off, since Taured isn't recognized as a country and thus doesn't have a stamp recognized in this reality construct to match it against. Maybe change to:

The passport contains stamps from airports located in Paris, Tokyo, The United States of America, and Germany, matching the ones used by the associated country. It also contains a stamp for the Taured National Airport.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I've mentioned in the forum, I like this SCP and glad it is brought to light.

In particular, I enjoy the ending. My headcanon(s) of it is as follows:

+ show block
– hide block

1. The Foundation of the 2 universes managed to get their guy back to their respective home realities.

2. There was no "real portal to the other universe". The ashes brought back are the same as the one they brought in. The tunnel caused that man to become his alternate counterpart (and maybe vice versa?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've made the change you suggest and i'm looking through it again for situation where i can re-work the sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said in the forums I like it, however I am noticing a few tone breaks/phrasing errors, i.e. "from a country called Taured" should probably be "from a nation known as Taured". Also you tend to use a lot of small, choppy sentences when they could be more easily combined into larger ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The line-breaks were just a formatting mistake i made. I'll fix that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll edit this later with a more in-depth look, but overall it wasn't that exciting. It's written fairly enough but it failed to really connect with me much. I understand the emotions you wanted to get out of this and what kind of pull you were trying to make, but it just didn't happen with me.

Also: why have line breaks in-between the start of sections and the text? For example:

Description:

SCP-2516-1 was a male human of Eastern European descent.

Instead of:

Description: SCP-2516-1 was a male human of Eastern European descent.

And so-on throughout the text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never really felt it. It was like a softer Republic of Arnold Fitzwilliams, and never as intriguing to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out. I've made the changes you suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you have any ideas on how I could add to it? I know feedback isn't required but I'd appreciate any suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are some comma splices in this that jar me out of the story.

SCP-2516-1 is deceased, the remains of SCP-2516-1 have been disposed of.

A recording of an early interview with SCP-2516-1 has also been included in the document, the conversation has been translated from French:

Those commas should either be semicolons or periods, with the second half of the sentences becoming their own, stand-alone sentences.

Hope this is helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My interpretation is that he was, sorta. His ashes transferred over and the Foundation-equivalent in his universe returned the ashes of the person he had switched with.

Overall, I find this a pretty meh article. I get that you were trying to evoke a sense of homesickness, but this just fell flat for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a stylistic choice, but you don't *have* to blackbox the name if you don't want. Just make up a name. I assume you already know what's behind the blackbox, so you could just use that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was pretty ho-hum for me right up to the final addendum. That last bit just made it all so sad for me, that he couldn't even be returned to his home in death. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surprised you're the first to do this (far as I'm aware). I always found that story fascinating (despite, sadly, not a lot of good information to support it once you dig deeper:()

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're right, the black boxes fit better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. I think black boxes might work better, but now that I get it, it doesn't seem as odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The data expunged was his name. I wasn't sure to use that, black boxes, or just the designation number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. The data expunged at the end of the interview doesn't seem necessary, but it could just be me missing something. Overall, +1
